text:
"Your shirt’s hanging off you again—I can see it from here. At least eat this apple on your way!"
"Thank you, Mrs. Mellow. I’ll pay you on the way back!"
She only shook her head. Caleb slid the apple into his bag and ran on. He tried to flag down a two-horse mail coach heading for the shipping company’s headquarters, but it didn’t stop; the old postman on the box might not have seen him at all.

Under other circumstances he’d have been glad to walk. The harbor was close, and the sea’s salt was strong in the air. On workday mornings he liked to take a detour, eat his breakfast to the slap of waves and the gulls’ cries. Today he had to settle for two deep breaths and the distant carry of ropes, winches, and sailors’ shouts. It could have been worse. At least it wasn’t raining, and there was no mud to cling to his shoes and complete the sorry picture.

By the time he reached the Western Shipping Company’s modern offices, sweat was running down his back. He didn’t bother to check his pocket watch to see how late he was. He hurried up the steps into the maroon-carpeted hall and asked at the desk where the hearing was.

He took the stairs to the first-floor conference room and paused at the door to catch his breath. After the sprint, he wanted his head clear—he had to back Lord Ebbing. Voices carried from inside; they had started without him. Not that he was anyone they’d wait for. He drew a long breath and pressed the big brass handle.

“You told us it was safe, Lord Ebbing!” Lord Denver slammed the table—one of the Western Shipping Company’s leading directors, and owner of the most recently vanished ship, the Daughter of the Wind. He was nearer eighty than seventy, but tall and straight-backed, his hair gone snow-white and his blue eyes cold. Like many old men he wore his beard long, trimmed by the best barbers into a severe, squared shape. When Caleb and Lord Ebbing had gone over what to expect, both had guessed they’d be facing him. Kindness seemed unlikely.

There were several others around the table. Besides Lord Denver, Caleb recognized Mr. Powlett, a thin man in his forties whose skin looked like yellow wax beginning to soften in the heat of the hearth. He oversaw the financial side of trade with the Colonies but, like Lord Ebbing, had a scholar’s heart. Mr. Nealy was there as well—father to William, one of Caleb’s good friends. Like his son, his flame-red hair already held grey, but warmth shone in his brown eyes. Two other men were strangers, bringing the committee to five. In the corner at a small separate table sat a lanky, hawk-nosed secretary, her typewriter clacking as she took notes.

“I said probably safe, Lord Denver. I have statistics, not command of the sea,” Lord Ebbing shot back, just as heated. Caleb’s immediate superior and mentor was a short, stout man in his sixties, bald on top as if his hair had migrated to the sides of his face where he kept it as whiskers, his chin clean-shaven—an exact inversion of Lord Denver. His head was flushed now, his brow shiny with sweat. Caleb had been there when the summons arrived and had seen how hard the Daughter of the Wind’s disappearance had hit Ebbing. Being called to account only made him more taut.

“So you’re saying you know nothing at all, and we should have drunk the funds we gave your research instead? Mrs. Sweets, note that down.”
“No, don’t. Mr. André—show them what you brought!”

Caleb flinched. He hadn’t known Ebbing had seen him come in; apparently he had, and hadn’t spared him even a nod in the heat of the quarrel. Caleb stepped forward and took the folded calendar from his case. Mr. Nealy hurried to help him fasten it to a large wooden board.

On it, green dots marked sailings that had completed the round trip; blue, those reported to have reached their destination but never returned; red, the ships that had vanished. Seen at a distance, the calendar showed an unmistakable pattern, outliers aside. At least until the past year.

“Good morning, gentlemen,” Caleb began, heart thudding as on every exam, though he’d graduated years ago. “As you can see, Lord Ebbing and I conducted an extensive study of the Western Ocean’s navigability. We didn’t only look at sailings out of Rivenstorm—we corresponded with Scalmoor and Einfjalla, and asked the Academy in Tineburgh to send over any records they had or could compile. The result is here. Although this chart shows only the past five years, the pattern runs much further back.

“If you look, every third new moon and through the month that follows, there are only rare disappearances. We treated those as outliers. Losses cluster heavily in the two weeks before those third new moons. Taken together, the data are cyclical. That cannot be chance.” He glanced at Lord Ebbing, who gave a small nod—go on. “On that basis our department forecast the optimal launch windows, when ships were most likely to make the run to the Colonies and back, accounting for ordinary weather variation as well.

“However, here you can see…” Caleb bent to indicate the panel showing the last year at the bottom of the poster. “…the pattern has begun to break apart. First here in Rivenstorm, then in our correspondents’ ports as well, as if the disharmony were spreading to other routes.”

He fell silent. Every eye was on him, but that was as far as he and Lord Ebbing had planned.

“And from this, Mr. André, you conclude…?” Lord Denver prompted, fairly enough. He wanted a plan. A proposal to mend the breach.

“I conclude we need more data, in case a new pattern is emerging. In earlier years Lord Ebbing’s recommendation worked—every port lost far fewer ships than before—and we advised the Daughter of the Wind’s departure on the same basis. Which, I would note, Lord Denver, you did not follow to the letter.”

The man flushed.

“And my ship was to sit idle another month in harbor? Do you know what that costs, boy?” His voice rose, but Lord Ebbing cut in.

“Undoubtedly a great deal, but Mr. André is correct. The date was not before the third new moon but the second, where…” Lord Ebbing rose. “…the old pattern did show fewer disappearances. Yet with the new data we could no longer state that the Daughter of the Wind would certainly return. At sea nothing is ever certain—storms see to that—but had you waited until after the third new moon, I suspect your odds would have been better.”

“How dare you—!”
